narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and academic rankings to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 23, Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, and plans to attend full-time—and predicted a 44% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass despite being below the typical 3.21 average. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which significantly pulled the prediction in the opposite direction toward failure, standing well below the dataset average of 36.7 and signaling potential challenges in legal reasoning under exam conditions. Your Year 3 decile ranking of 3, also below the average of about 5.5, had a milder but still helpful effect, nudging the model slightly toward anticipating success compared to lower performers. Overall, while your GPA provided a solid foundation relative to many peers, the low LSAT outweighed it as the dominant concern, tipping the balance against admission in a competitive pool where typical successful applicants score much higher on standardized tests. Your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 90%), so it didn't sway the decision much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a middle-income background—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted preparation—but the model saw your metrics as riskier than the average student's stronger test performance and rankings. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above 36, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your resilience and any upward trends in performance. Schools value persistence, and addressing these key gaps could flip the prediction in your favor. You're capable of this—many applicants in your position have turned rejections into acceptances by focusing on these exact areas. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
